message:
perf(vdbe): take agg-step args by slice when registers are in-place

`AggStep` was unconditionally cloning the argument registers into an
owned `Vec<SqliteValue>` via `collect_reg_range(op.p2, op.p5)` before
running the accumulator step. That's a lot of cloning on the inner
loop of any GROUP BY query: for every row × every aggregate, we
allocate, copy out the values, run the step, and drop. For aggregates
that just read their args (which is most of them — SUM, COUNT, MIN,
MAX, AVG, the JSON aggregates, every user-defined aggregate that
doesn't mutate its inputs), the clone is pure waste.

Two changes:

1. **Slice fast path.** When `op.p2 >= 0` and the requested arg range
   fits inside `self.registers`, borrow the args directly out of the
   register array as `&[SqliteValue]` and pass that slice into the
   accumulator step. The downstream API (`func.step(state, args)`,
   `agg_step_min_max_collated`, `distinct_key_collated`) was already
   slice-shaped on the inside; this commit just stops materializing
   the owning `Vec` before reaching them.

   We still call `materialize_make_record_sideband` for each register
   in the range before borrowing, because some operands carry deferred
   sideband state (MakeRecord coalescing, blob materialization) that
   has to be flushed before the slice is read. That part is unchanged
   semantically; just hoisted up.

2. **Cold-path fallback.** If the bounds check fails (negative p2 or
   the range overflows `self.registers.len()`), we fall back to the
   old `collect_reg_range` path so we don't change the error
   semantics for ill-formed bytecode. In practice this path should
   never run on bytecode produced by the planner — it's a safety
   harness in case a fuzzer or a hand-rolled VDBE program asks for
   an out-of-range read.

   The fallback path lives in the same `else` arm as the fast path
   and dispatches to the shared helper described next.

To make both call sites use the same accumulator logic without
duplicating the `entry_or_insert_with` + DISTINCT + cancellation +
min/max-collated fast path code, the body of the aggregate step has
been extracted into a new private associated function:

    fn agg_step_with_args(
        cold_state: &mut Option<Box<ColdVdbeState>>,
        statement_cold_state: &mut StatementColdState,
        accum_reg: i32,
        is_distinct: bool,
        func: &Arc<ErasedAggregateFunction>,
        func_name: &str,
        agg_collation: Option<&str>,
        execution_cx: &Cx,
        args: &[SqliteValue],
    ) -> Result<()>

Notes on the helper signature:

- It takes `&mut self`'s relevant fields explicitly rather than
  `&mut self`. That's because the slice-fast-path arm borrows
  `self.registers` immutably for the duration of the call, which
  conflicts with re-borrowing `&mut self` for the cold-state
  mutation. Splitting the borrow at field granularity sidesteps
  the conflict without resorting to unsafe or to copying the args
  back into an owned vec just to satisfy the borrow checker.

- It calls `statement_cold_state.insert(StatementColdState::
  AGGREGATES)` itself rather than relying on the previous
  `ensure_cold_state_for(...)` helper, since the helper also
  takes `&mut self` and would fight the same borrow.

- `execution_cx` is passed by reference (`&Cx`); the previous
  call site cloned the Arc once and called
  `observe_execution_cancellation(&execution_cx)`. The new helper
  observes cancellation both before and after the step (matching
  the prior order) but takes an immutable reference for both
  observations.

The DISTINCT bookkeeping, the cross-aggregate accumulator-misuse
check (Arc::ptr_eq), the NULL-skip for DISTINCT, the
`agg_step_min_max_collated` fast path, and the cancellation observers
are all preserved bit-for-bit. This is a refactor for ownership and
allocation, not a behavior change.

Steady-state effect: GROUP BY-heavy reads stop allocating per
aggregate-step on the hot path. Allocation count in the inner loop
of `SELECT a, sum(b), avg(c) FROM t GROUP BY a` drops from "two vecs
per row per aggregate" (one for collect_reg_range, one for the
DISTINCT key) to "one only when DISTINCT" — a meaningful improvement
on long aggregations.
